summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Where litigation funding by way of partial property settlement is sought by the wife – Where the wife seeks the sale of the former matrimonial home – Where neither the husband nor the wife seek to retain the former matrimonial home at trial – Where the husband has been living in the former matrimonial home since separation without paying rent – Where the wife has been paying rent since separation – Where the hearing date was adjourned at no fault of the wife but at least in part the fault of the husband – Where the order sought does not exceed the wife’s entitlement to property settlement – Where it is just and equitable to make an order for a partial property settlement – Where the wife is not granted sole use and occupation of the former matrimonial home pending sale.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

STRUM J:

On 19 February 2026, the Applicant wife filed a further amended Application for Final Orders. Under the rubric of “final orders sought”, she sought certain orders in the event the trial listed to commence on 10 March 2026 was adjourned.
By orders made on 24 February 2026, the trial date was vacated and the application for those orders, properly being in the nature of interlocutory relief, was deemed to be an Application in a Proceeding, which was made returnable this day.
The orders sought by the wife include the sale of the former matrimonial home and the payment to her of the sum of $300,000, from the proceeds of sale, by way of partial property settlement, primarily to fund her litigation costs in these proceedings.
The application is opposed by the Respondent husband, not because he contends that the wife will receive less than $300,000 upon final determination of the parties’ competing applications for property settlement, but because he contends that it is not just and equitable to make a partial property settlement order at this stage of the proceedings.

BACKGROUND

The husband is 79 years of age. The wife is 74 years of age and is in remission from cancer for the second time. They commenced cohabitation in 1970, were married in 1984, and separated in July of 2022. There are five adult children of the marriage, including the second respondent, who is the effective owner and controller of the fourth respondent, a corporation.
It is fair to say that the wife is pitted in these proceedings against the husband and the second respondent and their companies, and that the husband and the second respondent appear to have a common interest in these proceedings. Not only does the husband not dispute the second respondent’s claims in large part but, by his amended Response to Initiating Application filed 3 February 2026, he expressly seeks that the pool of assets available for division between the wife and him be confined to the real properties not the subject of the second respondent’s claims.
This is so notwithstanding that, in respect of the two other real properties, the second respondent claims that he has a lease over one property and a life interest in the other, such that those properties remain the property of the husband and the wife via their corporate entities, subject to the second respondent’s claimed interests. The clarification and resolution of that incongruity must await trial. The lease granted by the husband to the second respondent over one of the real properties is for a term of 99 years at a rental of $1 per annum. It is the subject of an application by the wife pursuant to s 106B of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth).
Taking the husband’s case at its highest, he contends that the two real properties available for division between the wife and him are the former matrimonial home situate at E Street, Suburb D, valued at $1.1 million, and a commercial property situate at 2 G Street, Suburb H, which is subject to an arms-length commercial lease and valued at $2.775 million. Both properties are unencumbered and they have a total combined value of $3.875 million.
The husband proposes an even division of the property of the wife and him. She seeks a division of 55 per cent in her favour and 45 per cent in his favour. Therefore, on the husband’s case, the wife will receive not less than $1.937 million. It is common ground that neither party seeks to retain the former matrimonial home at trial. The husband seeks to retain the corporate entity which is the registered proprietor of the undisputed commercial entity at 2 G Street, Suburb H.

LEGAL PRINCIPLES

The principles applicable to application are well settled. In Strahan & Strahan (Interim Property Orders) [2011] FamCAFC 126; (2011) FLC 93-466 at [118], the Full Court said:
... There are two stages to the hearing of such an application where the power is to be exercised pursuant to s 80(1)(h) of the Act. This is recognised by the fact that although the power under s 79 should ordinarily be exercised on a once only basis, “circumstances may arise before there can be a final hearing” where the power is exercised. Thus the first step is to resolve whether to exercise the power before a final hearing and if it is resolved to do so then the second step involves the exercise of that power.
The Full Court earlier said at [115]:
The first consideration is the adjectival or procedural step and the second consideration is the substantive step. The first step requires consideration as to whether the jurisdiction will be entertained. The second step arises if the jurisdiction is entertained and requires consideration of the factors which are relevant to the exercise of power under s 79 to make an order.
The Full Court continued at [132]:
In relation to the first stage, in our view, when considering whether to exercise the power under s 79 and s 80(1)(h) of the Act to make an interim property order the “overarching consideration” is the interests of justice. It is not necessary to establish compelling circumstances. All that is required is that in the circumstances it is appropriate to exercise the power. In exercising the wide and unfettered discretion conferred by the power to make such an order, regard should be had to the fact that the usual order pursuant to s 79 is a once and for all order made after a final hearing.
The Full Court said at [135]:
In relation to the second matter, as the jurisdiction under s 79 of the Act is being exercised the provisions of that section must be considered and applied but with limitations given that it is not the final hearing. There is also no requirement of compelling circumstances in relation to the substantive step.
The wife seeks the sale of the former matrimonial home in circumstances where it is conceded by the husband that it will be sold at trial. She submits that it is just and equitable to do so at this juncture in the proceedings. In respect of the quantum, her costs notice filed 17 March 2026 discloses that she has paid legal costs of $200,373.57 from her personal funds which, it is common ground, have their genesis in an inheritance received by her some years ago of approximately $500,000.
Moreover, from that inheritance, the wife has lent or advanced the further sum of $200,000 to the second respondent; however, he disputes any liability to repay that sum and therefore it is not presently available to her. The balance of her inheritance, in the order of approximately $100,000, is (or was) primarily in her National Australia Bank account ending #01. As at the date of her Financial Statement filed on 15 January 2026, that account had a balance of $108,427. Exhibit W1, tendered this day, discloses that, as at 26 February 2026, the balance of that account decreased to $103,427, including by reason of a transfer by her to another account in her name ending #04 which had a balance of $2,203 as at 18 March 2026 (Exhibit W2).
The balance of the wife’s account ending #01 was further reduced two days ago by a payment of $22,000, on account of her Senior Counsel’s fees. I consider it is entirely appropriate to retain Senior Counsel given the number and complexity of the issues in this case. I therefore find that the wife presently has in the order of $81,500 at her disposal.
The wife’s costs notice discloses that she owes her lawyers sum of $60,650 and that her estimated costs of this day are $35,200 of which, I infer, she has already paid $22,000, by reason of the payment to her Senior Counsel, reducing that amount to $13,200. Further, her estimated future costs and disbursements are said to total $257,000. Therefore, she has owing as at today, and/or by reason of today, together with her future estimated legal costs, a sum of approximately $331,000, of which she has $81,500 in the bank. Accordingly, she needs approximately $250,000 to fund the balance of her legal costs, without making any provision for any other incidental expenses to meet the vicissitudes of life, and she seeks a payment of $300,000.

APPLICATION

The husband does not cavil with the proposition that the wife will receive at least $300,000 or that her present and future costs, as estimated, are unreasonable. That deals with the second or substantive step to which the Full Court in Strahan & Strahan refers. I turn, therefore, to what is the gravamen in this case, which the Full Court in Strahan & Strahan refers to as the adjectival step, namely, whether it is just and equitable to order a sale at this juncture.
In this regard, the plurality of the High Court of Australia in Stanford & Stanford [2012] HCA 52; (2012) 247 CLR 108 said at [36]:
The expression “just and equitable” is a qualitative description of a conclusion reached after examination of a range of potentially competing considerations. It does not admit of exhaustive definition. It is not possible to chart its metes and bounds. ...

(Footnote omitted)
The husband and the wife separated in mid-2022, nearly four years ago, when the wife left the former matrimonial home. She has lived in rented accommodation since then. Her financial statement discloses that she pays $471 per week by way of rent. That equates to approximately $25,000 per annum or $2,000 per month. Her rent and other living expenses, she deposes, are funded from her savings, as well as from the sum of approximately $3,000 per month paid to her by the husband.
Her Financial Statement discloses income of $696 per week received from the husband, pursuant to orders made on 1 March 2023, and expenses in the order of $1,550 per week. Those expenses are not the subject of any real criticism by counsel for the husband, and sensibly so. Therefore, I find that the wife has a shortfall in the order of $854 per week, which, of necessity, is funded from her savings. She also has the legal expenses detailed above.
Meanwhile, whilst the wife has been paying rent for nearly four years, the husband has continued to live rent-free in the former matrimonial home purchased in 2017 by the wife and him. In Strahan & Strahan at [80], the Full Court said:
In Poletti and Poletti (unreported, Family Court of Australia, 2 March 1990) Nygh J, when describing an application for “interim costs”, referred to the reasons of the Full Court in Wilson and Wilson [1989] FamCA 34; (1989) FLC 92-033 (“Wilson”) and said it is a “situation where one party to the marriage controls almost exclusively what might be described as the patrimony of the parties and has control of the bulk of the assets and funds of the parties, where an order may be made to ensure that the other party, who does not have the fortune of controlling those funds, at least has an equal or near equal opportunity to present his or her case” ...
In this case, putting to one side, for present purposes, the real properties of the husband and wife’s corporate entities, in respect of which the son claims a leasehold or life interest, the husband controls almost exclusively, and enjoys the use and benefit of, the patrimony of assets of the wife and him, save as referred to below.
It is uncontentious that these real properties, at 2 G Street and 3 G Street, Suburb H, have provided the funds referred to in the orders made by consent on 1 March 2023, pursuant to which, from rent of $9,660 per calendar month, the sum of $3,016.92 is paid to the wife. Otherwise, the husband has the control and use of $6,643 per calendar month (approximately $1,533 per week).
However, in his Financial Statement, the husband inexplicably only discloses income of $700 per week. The discrepancy might have been thought to be entirely referable to the outgoings and expenses of C Pty Ltd, which is the registered proprietor of the two properties. However, in his affidavit filed 5 March 2026, the husband refers to the orders made on 1 March 2023 and confirms that the rent therefrom is $9,660 per month. He further deposes that the wife receives therefrom the fixed sum of $3,016.92 per month, and that the balance of the rental income is retained by him, from which he meets his living costs and legal costs, and maintains the former matrimonial home, without any reference to the costs of 1 G Street and 2 G Street, Suburb H.
The husband does not point to any other reasonable source from which the wife can meet her legal costs. As the second respondent denies any obligation to repay the $200,000 advanced or lent to him by the wife, she presently has no recourse to that amount. I observe, parenthetically, that the husband’s costs notice, filed for the purposes this hearing, estimates present and future $130,000. However, the wife is battling five respondents who make a common cause in this Court.
The husband opposes the sale of the former matrimonial home at this juncture because he says that will require him to move and to pay for a bond and rent. However, on his case, he will need to do that anyway, after trial, because he does not seek to retain the property. Therefore, he seeks to delay the inevitable, whilst the wife is presently shut out of the assets which either the husband controls or to which their son, the second respondent, lays claim.
Although the husband opposes the orders sought by the wife, he does not propose any alternative orders in the event the wife is successful in relation to the sale of the former matrimonial home. He adopts a binary approach. However, he could have sought a partial property settlement also in his favour, in the event he were unsuccessful, but he did not do so. He could, likewise, have sought specific terms and conditions in the event a sale of the former matrimonial home was ordered but did not do so.
The husband contends that the wife should await the trial; however, the trial was listed to commence earlier this month and was adjourned at no fault of her own. The husband very belatedly disclosed a sub-lease in respect of the property at 4 G Street, Suburb H, which is the subject of the disputed lease, notwithstanding longstanding and repeated requests by the wife for disclosure of any sub-leases. The sub-lease, in turn, is to an entity associated with the second respondent and his wife. That, at least in part, gave rise to the adjournment of the trial, the hearing of which would have concluded by now. The husband is therefore, at least in part, responsible for the adjournment.
There is some merit to the husband’s submission that the orders sought by the wife, insofar as they seek immediate vacation by him of the former of matrimonial home, would not be just and equitable. That can be addressed and alleviated by not acceding to that part of the wife’s application seeking that she have sole use and occupation forthwith, and by providing that, subject to any further interlocutory orders that might be made hereafter, he be permitted to continue to live in the former matrimonial home until settlement of the sale.
As referred to above, the difficulty for him is that he does not seek any orders of his own. However, sensibly, Senior Counsel for the wife advised the Court that the wife would not oppose the making of orders for the early release of a deposit if possible and that any request by the husband for a release of funds to him, either from the deposit or from the settlement proceeds, would be reasonably considered. In the event that the husband were required to make an application, and the Court were to find that the wife had unreasonably opposed any release of funds to him, that may result in costs consequences.
In all the circumstances, taking into account the very broad discretion reposed in me pursuant to s 79(2) of the Act, I am satisfied that it is just and equitable, at this juncture, to order a partial property settlement in favour the wife, and that the sum of $300,000 is appropriate, to be funded by the sale of the former matrimonial home, subject to the husband presently remaining in occupation thereof (and subject to any orders that might need to be made in that regard hereafter), and to the parties doing all acts and things to request the early release of the deposit to the wife’s solicitors, to be held upon trust for them pending written agreement or further order.
I do not consider it appropriate to make an order of the kind referred to by the Full Court in Bing & Bing [2007] FamCA 418; (2007) FLC 93-318 and to leave the source of payment as a later question of enforcement. That would likely lead to further interlocutory applications and, in any event, the husband cannot point to, or does not propose, any other source of funding for the wife.
I understand why the wife seeks the immediate sole use and occupation of the former matrimonial home, given the breaches and conduct alleged by her. However, findings in that regards cannot be made at this interlocutory stage. Further, I will not proceed upon the speculative basis of an anticipatory breach; orders of the Court are not aspirational, they are binding and are to be complied with.
If the husband seeks to interfere with the sale process, as the wife fears, and a further application is required to be made to the Court, the consequences may then include an order granting sole use and occupation of the former matrimonial home to the wife, and the exclusion of the husband therefrom, as well as costs.
In the absence of alternative orders sought by the husband, including as to the terms and conditions of the sale of the former matrimonial home, I shall make orders substantially in the form sought by the wife.
